title: Human cost-expression effect size is 16% of E. coli's — far larger than the naive Ne≈10^-4 prediction, placing human cost selection in the strong-selection regime

subject.display: Human cost-expression effect size is 16% of E. coli's — far larger than the naive Ne≈10^-4 prediction, placing human cost selection in the strong-selection regime

parts.prose: Constituent claim cost_expression_gradient_human of the composite synthesis: The ratio of cost-expression effect sizes (human vs E. coli + B. subtilis) is consistent with effective-population-size scaling, proposing a causal selection mechanism for the dual-channel hypothesis.

Constituent claim cost_expression_gradient_ecoli_bsubtilis of the composite synthesis: The ratio of cost-expression effect sizes (human vs E. coli + B. subtilis) is consistent with effective-population-size scaling, proposing a causal selection mechanism for the dual-channel hypothesis.

source.note: MOD-3 effect-size table: per-species (Q1 − Q5) ATP/residue deltas for human, E. coli, B. subtilis. Staged from results/mod03_effect_size_context.log.
Long-term effective population sizes: Ne_human ≈ 10^4 (Takahata 1993; Lynch 2007), Ne_E_coli ≈ 10^8 (Bobay & Ochman 2018). Curated constants, not pulled from the live API.

operations.note: Pull the human and E. coli (Q1 − Q5) percentages from the MOD-3 effect-size table.
Observed ratio = human_delta_pct / ecoli_delta_pct ≈ 1.3 / 2.5 = 0.158 per the compiled paper.
Naive prediction: effect size ∝ Ne under weak-selection; human Ne / E. coli Ne ≈ 10^4 / 10^8 = 1e-4. Observed / predicted ≈ 1580×.

statistics.name: observed_human_over_ecoli_q1q5_pct_ratio
statistics.note: Observed ratio from MOD-3 log: `human_effect / ecoli_effect = 0.158`. Equivalently: the human effect is 16% of the E. coli effect.

statistics.name: predicted_human_over_ecoli_from_linear_ne
statistics.note: Naive linear-Ne prediction: 10^4 / 10^8 = 1e-4. This is the null prediction for cost optimization under weak selection; the observed effect being ~1580× larger is the load-bearing surprise.

statistics.name: observed_over_ne_prediction
statistics.note: Derived: 0.158 / 1e-4 = 1580. The human effect is three orders of magnitude above the weak-selection expectation, consistent with the strong-selection regime (|Ne·s| >> 1) even at Ne ≈ 10^4.

inference.justification: Two conjuncts encode the Ne-scaling surprise: (1) observed > predicted, stat-vs-stat, confirms the direction of the departure from the weak-selection null; (2) observed/predicted > 100 ensures the departure is orders-of-magnitude large, ruling out small-number-game artifacts (e.g., effect-size measurement error of ≤ 2× could not produce a 1000× departure). Together they place human cost selection in the strong-selection regime.
inference.failure_mode: If the observed ratio fell to within a factor of ~10 of the Ne prediction, the weak-selection null could not be rejected; cost optimization would then be consistent with drift-dominated dynamics at Ne ≈ 10^4. The load-bearing margin is the ~1580× excess.

conclusion.assertion: The observed human/E. coli cost-expression effect-size ratio (0.158, i.e. human effect is 16% of E. coli) is ~1580× larger than the weak-selection null prediction of 10^-4 from their ~10^4-fold Ne ratio. Cost-optimizing selection on protein coding sequences therefore operates in the strong-selection regime in humans, not in the drift-dominated regime that a naive Ne scaling would predict.

scope.scale_note: Ratio-level claim across two species. The within-species gradient claims are separate fixtures (`cost_expression_gradient_human`, `cost_expression_gradient_ecoli_bsubtilis`). This claim is the interpretive synthesis.

confidence.note: No significance test — this is an order-of-magnitude comparison between observed and naive-null effect-size ratios.

external_assumptions.statement: Ne_human ≈ 10^4 and Ne_E.coli ≈ 10^8 are accurate long-term effective population sizes for the purpose of weak-vs-strong selection regime classification.

Linear Ne scaling of cost-selection effect size is the correct weak-selection null; this assumes selection coefficients are comparable across species, an approximation that hides per-residue physiology.
The observed-vs-predicted ratio is robust to ±2× uncertainty in either effect-size measurement or in either Ne estimate; a 1580× departure cannot be explained by such ambiguity.
